sections.text: Every single page on the internet has its own unique address — just like every house has its own street address. These web addresses are called URLs. Learning to read a URL is like learning to read a map — once you understand the parts, you can navigate anywhere on the internet!

sections.title: What is a URL? 📍
sections.text: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator. That's a fancy way of saying 'the address of something on the web.' A URL tells your browser three main things: what rules to use to get there (the protocol), where to go (the domain), and exactly what to pick up (the path).

sections.title: Anatomy of a URL 🔬
sections.text: Let's break down this URL: https://www.example.com:443/blog/post-1?color=blue#section2 — Every part means something specific!

sections.title: The Parts of a URL
items: https:// — The Protocol. Rules for communication. (http or https)
www — The Subdomain. A section of the domain. (www is the most common, but you can have blog.example.com, shop.example.com, etc.)
example.com — The Domain Name. The main address of the website.
.com — The TLD (Top Level Domain). Could be .com, .org, .net, .io, etc.
:443 — The Port. Usually hidden — 443 is default for HTTPS, 80 for HTTP.
/blog/post-1 — The Path. Which specific page or file you want.
?color=blue — The Query String. Extra information sent to the page (after the ?).
#section2 — The Fragment. Jumps you to a specific part of the page.

sections.title: URL as a Home Address 🏠
sections.text: Think of it like a physical address. 'https' is like saying 'I'll walk there' (the mode of travel). 'example.com' is the city and street. '/blog/post-1' is the specific apartment number. '?color=blue' is a note you hand to the person at the door. '#section2' is 'go straight to the kitchen when you get inside.'

sections.title: What is a Domain Name? 🏷️
sections.text: The domain name is the human-readable name you register to own a piece of the internet. You can buy domain names from registrars (companies that sell them) for usually a few dollars a year. Popular registrars include GoDaddy, Namecheap, and Google Domains. Once you own a domain, you point it to your server using DNS.

sections.title: Absolute vs Relative URLs
sections.text: An absolute URL includes everything — the full address from start to finish, like 'https://google.com/maps'. A relative URL only includes the path, like '/maps' — it assumes you're already on the same website. In code, you'll use relative URLs a lot when linking between pages of your own site!
🌍 Fun Fact: There are over 350 million registered domain names in the world! The very first domain ever registered was 'symbolics.com' in 1985 — and it still exists today.

terms.definition: Uniform Resource Locator — the full address of a page or file on the web.
The first part of a URL (http/https) — the rules for communication.
The human-friendly name of a website (example.com).
The part after the domain that points to a specific page or file (/blog/post-1).
Extra data passed in the URL after a ? mark (?color=blue).
A prefix before the domain (like 'www' or 'blog' in blog.example.com).

questions.explanation: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator — it's just a fancy name for a web address. Every page on the internet has a unique URL.

questions.text: In the URL 'https://shop.example.com/products?sort=price', what is 'shop' called?
questions.explanation: 'shop' is a subdomain — it's a section of the main domain 'example.com'. Subdomains let you have different sections of a site at different addresses, like shop.example.com or blog.example.com.
'/news/article-5' is the path — it tells the server which specific page or resource you want. Think of it like an apartment number within a building (the domain).

questions.text: What does the '#section2' at the end of a URL do?
options: Jumps you to a specific section of the page
questions.explanation: The fragment (#section2) is a client-side anchor — it doesn't get sent to the server at all! Instead, the browser uses it to scroll you to a specific element on the page that has that ID.
